A committee is evaluating a proposal built around repeated filtering. They agree its central unresolved question is precise: does repeated filtering ever produce genuinely new explanatory primitives, or does it only recombine inherited primitives with increasing efficiency? This means any apparent success must be interpreted under that ambiguity. A filtered output might look novel while still being only a sharper rearrangement of what was already present in the starting stock.

That ambiguity becomes harder to resolve because the system also depends on pools. If there is a universal pool in the background, it offers broad coverage, but its breadth creates a cost: it can smooth over rare, domain-specific irregularities. Yet those irregularities may be exactly what real discovery requires. So if the proposal favors universal pools, then any failure to discover genuinely strange but true structure could be blamed on flattening. If it favors novel pools to preserve weirdness, then it risks losing the stabilizing reach of the universal background. The tension is structural, not accidental.

To test the proposal, the committee uses the Einstein benchmark. But they know why that benchmark is attractive: success conditions are already settled. This makes the benchmark easy to score and easy to compare. At the same time, that convenience contaminates interpretation. Because everyone already knows what counts as success, the benchmark can reward retrieval of a recognized form rather than production of new knowledge. So even a strong result on the Einstein benchmark would not settle the proposal’s main unresolved question. It could show that repeated filtering is good at converging on known explanatory patterns without showing that it creates new primitives.

While this methodological dispute is ongoing, a separate political disturbance occurs: the controversial representatives retreated after someone tossed the cupboard. Whatever coalition or forum they occupied is thereby altered by retreat under disruption. Afterward, trim control over the inexplicable smuggled goods returned. So following the retreat and the cupboard incident, there is again narrow or reduced control over smuggled goods whose character remains unexplained.

Taken together, the world described is one in which both epistemic and institutional control are unstable. Epistemically, the proposal cannot yet prove that filtering creates novelty rather than reorganizing inheritance, and its preferred benchmark risks mistaking recognition for discovery. Institutionally, a disturbance causes controversial representatives to withdraw, and the aftermath restores only trim control over inexplicable smuggled goods, not a full explanation of them.

So the likely condition of this world is not resolution but constrained management. The evaluators can continue testing, but their best-known benchmark cannot answer their deepest question. The institutions can reassert some control after disruption, but only over goods still not understood. In both cases, procedure returns before explanation does.
